question:
3. Find an integral formula for the solution of the differential equation
\[
\delta(\delta-1)(\delta-2) \cdots(\delta-n+1) y=f(x), \quad x \geq 1
\]
for \( y \) as a function of \( x \) satisfying the initial conditions \( y(1)=y^{\prime}(1)=\ldots= \) \( y^{(n-1)}(1)=0 \), where \( f \) is continuous and
\[
\delta \equiv x \frac{d}{d x}
\]
solution:
Solution. We first show that
\[
\delta(\delta-1)(\delta-2) \cdots(\delta-n+1) y=x^{n} \frac{d^{n} y}{d x^{n}}
\]

We prove this by induction on \( n \). It is true for \( n=1 \). Assume (1) is true for \( n=k \). Since polynomials in the operator \( \delta \) commute with one another, we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
\delta(\delta-1)(\delta-2) \cdots(\delta-k & +1)(\delta-k) y \\
& =(\delta-k) \delta(\delta-1)(\delta-2) \cdots(\delta-k+1) y \\
& =\left(x \frac{d}{d x}-k\right) x^{k} \frac{d^{k} y}{d x^{k}} \\
& =x^{k+1} \frac{d^{k+1} y}{d x^{k+1}}
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus (1) is proved by induction for all \( n \).
The differential equation is thus
\[
x^{n} y^{(n)}=f(x), \quad x \geq 1
\]
and the solution can obviously be obtained by applying the integral operator \( \int_{1}^{x} n \) times to the function \( f(x) x^{-n} \). However, it is possible to collapse the \( n \)-fold integration to a single integration as follows:

One of the standard forms of Taylor's theorem is
\[
\begin{aligned}
y(x)=y(a)+(x-a) y^{\prime}(a)+\cdots+\frac{(x-a)^{n-1}}{(n-1)!} & y^{(n-1)}(a) \\
& +\int_{a}^{x} \frac{(x-t)^{n-1}}{(n-1)!} y^{(n)}(t) d t
\end{aligned}
\]
if \( y^{(n)} \) is continuous. (See, for example, Thomas, Calculus and Analytic Geometry, alternate ed., Addison-Wesley, Reading, Mass., 1972, page 814.) In this case, taking \( a=1 \) and using (2) and the initial conditions, we have
\[
y(x)=\int_{1}^{x} \frac{(x-t)^{n-1}}{(n-1)!} \cdot \frac{f(t)}{t^{n}} d t
\]

It is easy to check by direct differentiation that the function defined by this integral does indeed satisfy the differential equation (2) and the initial conditions.

descriptive_long.question:
3. Find an integral formula for the solution of the differential equation
\[
diffop(diffop-1)(diffop-2) \cdots(diffop-orderint+1)\,yfunction = forcingfx(xcoordv), \quad xcoordv \geq 1
\]
for \( yfunction \) as a function of \( xcoordv \) satisfying the initial conditions \( yfunction(1)=yfunction^{\prime}(1)=\ldots= yfunction^{(orderint-1)}(1)=0 \), where \( forcingfx \) is continuous and
\[
diffop \equiv xcoordv \frac{d}{d xcoordv}
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. We first show that
\[
diffop(diffop-1)(diffop-2) \cdots(diffop-orderint+1)\,yfunction = xcoordv^{orderint} \frac{d^{orderint} yfunction}{d xcoordv^{orderint}}
\]
We prove this by induction on \( orderint \). It is true for \( orderint = 1 \). Assume (1) is true for \( orderint = indexstep \). Since polynomials in the operator \( diffop \) commute with one another, we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
diffop(diffop-1)(diffop-2) \cdots(diffop-indexstep & +1)(diffop-indexstep)\,yfunction \\
& =(diffop-indexstep)\,diffop(diffop-1)(diffop-2) \cdots(diffop-indexstep+1)\,yfunction \\
& =\left(xcoordv \frac{d}{d xcoordv}-indexstep\right)xcoordv^{indexstep} \frac{d^{indexstep} yfunction}{d xcoordv^{indexstep}} \\
& =xcoordv^{indexstep+1} \frac{d^{indexstep+1} yfunction}{d xcoordv^{indexstep+1}}
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus (1) is proved by induction for all \( orderint \).

The differential equation is thus
\[
xcoordv^{orderint} yfunction^{(orderint)} = forcingfx(xcoordv), \quad xcoordv \geq 1
\]
and the solution can obviously be obtained by applying the integral operator \( \int_{1}^{xcoordv} orderint \) times to the function \( forcingfx(xcoordv) xcoordv^{-orderint} \). However, it is possible to collapse the \( orderint \)-fold integration to a single integration as follows:

One of the standard forms of Taylor's theorem is
\[
\begin{aligned}
yfunction(xcoordv)=yfunction(taylorpt)&+(xcoordv-taylorpt)\,yfunction^{\prime}(taylorpt)+\cdots+\frac{(xcoordv-taylorpt)^{orderint-1}}{(orderint-1)!}\,yfunction^{(orderint-1)}(taylorpt) \\
&+\int_{taylorpt}^{xcoordv} \frac{(xcoordv-paramtime)^{orderint-1}}{(orderint-1)!}\,yfunction^{(orderint)}(paramtime)\,d\,paramtime
\end{aligned}
\]
if \( yfunction^{(orderint)} \) is continuous. (See, for example, Thomas, Calculus and Analytic Geometry, alternate ed., Addison-Wesley, Reading, Mass., 1972, page 814.) In this case, taking \( taylorpt = 1 \) and using (2) and the initial conditions, we have
\[
yfunction(xcoordv)=\int_{1}^{xcoordv} \frac{(xcoordv-paramtime)^{orderint-1}}{(orderint-1)!}\,\frac{forcingfx(paramtime)}{paramtime^{orderint}}\,d\,paramtime
\]
It is easy to check by direct differentiation that the function defined by this integral does indeed satisfy the differential equation (2) and the initial conditions.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
3. Find an integral formula for the solution of the differential equation
\[
moonlight(moonlight-1)(moonlight-2) \cdots(moonlight-afterglow+1) marigold=sandstone(wanderlust), \quad wanderlust \geq 1
\]
for \( marigold \) as a function of \( wanderlust \) satisfying the initial conditions \( marigold(1)=marigold^{\prime}(1)=\ldots= marigold^{(afterglow-1)}(1)=0 \), where \( sandstone \) is continuous and
\[
moonlight \equiv wanderlust \frac{d}{d wanderlust}
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. We first show that
\[
moonlight(moonlight-1)(moonlight-2) \cdots(moonlight-afterglow+1) marigold=wanderlust^{afterglow} \frac{d^{afterglow} marigold}{d wanderlust^{afterglow}}
\]

We prove this by induction on \( afterglow \). It is true for \( afterglow=1 \). Assume (1) is true for \( afterglow=buttercup \). Since polynomials in the operator \( moonlight \) commute with one another, we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
moonlight(moonlight-1)(moonlight-2) \cdots(moonlight-buttercup & +1)(moonlight-buttercup) marigold \\
& =(moonlight-buttercup) moonlight(moonlight-1)(moonlight-2) \cdots(moonlight-buttercup+1) marigold \\
& =\left(wanderlust \frac{d}{d wanderlust}-buttercup\right) wanderlust^{buttercup} \frac{d^{buttercup} marigold}{d wanderlust^{buttercup}} \\
& =wanderlust^{buttercup+1} \frac{d^{buttercup+1} marigold}{d wanderlust^{buttercup+1}}
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus (1) is proved by induction for all \( afterglow \).
The differential equation is thus
\[
wanderlust^{afterglow} marigold^{(afterglow)}=sandstone(wanderlust), \quad wanderlust \geq 1
\]
and the solution can obviously be obtained by applying the integral operator \( \int_{1}^{wanderlust} \) afterglow times to the function \( sandstone(wanderlust) wanderlust^{-afterglow} \). However, it is possible to collapse the afterglow-fold integration to a single integration as follows:

One of the standard forms of Taylor's theorem is
\[
\begin{aligned}
marigold(wanderlust)=marigold(crossroads)+(wanderlust-crossroads) marigold^{\prime}(crossroads)+\cdots+\frac{(wanderlust-crossroads)^{afterglow-1}}{(afterglow-1)!} & marigold^{(afterglow-1)}(crossroads) \\
& +\int_{crossroads}^{wanderlust} \frac{(wanderlust-blueberry)^{afterglow-1}}{(afterglow-1)!} marigold^{(afterglow)}(blueberry) d blueberry
\end{aligned}
\]
if \( marigold^{(afterglow)} \) is continuous. (See, for example, Thomas, Calculus and Analytic Geometry, alternate ed., Addison-Wesley, Reading, Mass., 1972, page 814.) In this case, taking \( crossroads=1 \) and using (2) and the initial conditions, we have
\[
marigold(wanderlust)=\int_{1}^{wanderlust} \frac{(wanderlust-blueberry)^{afterglow-1}}{(afterglow-1)!} \cdot \frac{sandstone(blueberry)}{blueberry^{afterglow}} d blueberry
\]

It is easy to check by direct differentiation that the function defined by this integral does indeed satisfy the differential equation (2) and the initial conditions.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
3. Find an integral formula for the solution of the differential equation
\[
stability(stability-1)(stability-2) \cdots(stability-uncountable+1) inputdata=staticval(constantv), \quad constantv \geq 1
\]
for \( inputdata \) as a function of \( constantv \) satisfying the initial conditions \( inputdata(1)=inputdata^{\prime}(1)=\ldots= \) \( inputdata^{(uncountable-1)}(1)=0 \), where \( staticval \) is continuous and
\[
stability \equiv constantv \frac{d}{d constantv}
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. We first show that
\[
stability(stability-1)(stability-2) \cdots(stability-uncountable+1) inputdata=constantv^{uncountable} \frac{d^{uncountable} inputdata}{d constantv^{uncountable}}
\]
We prove this by induction on \( uncountable \). It is true for \( uncountable=1 \). Assume (1) is true for \( uncountable=endindex \). Since polynomials in the operator \( stability \) commute with one another, we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
stability(stability-1)(stability-2) \cdots(stability-endindex & +1)(stability-endindex) inputdata \\
& =(stability-endindex) stability(stability-1)(stability-2) \cdots(stability-endindex+1) inputdata \\
& =\left(constantv \frac{d}{d constantv}-endindex\right) constantv^{endindex} \frac{d^{endindex} inputdata}{d constantv^{endindex}} \\
& =constantv^{endindex+1} \frac{d^{endindex+1} inputdata}{d constantv^{endindex+1}}
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus (1) is proved by induction for all \( uncountable \).
The differential equation is thus
\[
constantv^{uncountable} inputdata^{(uncountable)}=staticval(constantv), \quad constantv \geq 1
\]
and the solution can obviously be obtained by applying the integral operator \( \int_{1}^{constantv} uncountable \) times to the function \( staticval(constantv) constantv^{-uncountable} \). However, it is possible to collapse the \( uncountable \)-fold integration to a single integration as follows:

One of the standard forms of Taylor's theorem is
\[
\begin{aligned}
inputdata(constantv)=inputdata(targetpt)+(constantv-targetpt) inputdata^{\prime}(targetpt)+\cdots+\frac{(constantv-targetpt)^{uncountable-1}}{(uncountable-1)!} & inputdata^{(uncountable-1)}(targetpt) \\
& +\int_{targetpt}^{constantv} \frac{(constantv-spaceaxis)^{uncountable-1}}{(uncountable-1)!} inputdata^{(uncountable)}(spaceaxis) d spaceaxis
\end{aligned}
\]
if \( inputdata^{(uncountable)} \) is continuous. (See, for example, Thomas, Calculus and Analytic Geometry, alternate ed., Addison-Wesley, Reading, Mass., 1972, page 814.) In this case, taking \( targetpt=1 \) and using (2) and the initial conditions, we have
\[
inputdata(constantv)=\int_{1}^{constantv} \frac{(constantv-spaceaxis)^{uncountable-1}}{(uncountable-1)!} \cdot \frac{staticval(spaceaxis)}{spaceaxis^{uncountable}} d spaceaxis
\]
It is easy to check by direct differentiation that the function defined by this integral does indeed satisfy the differential equation (2) and the initial conditions.

garbled_string.question:
3. Find an integral formula for the solution of the differential equation
\[
fyhjqska(fyhjqska-1)(fyhjqska-2) \cdots(fyhjqska-khqrezop+1) wcntoqrb=pzejxlym(vdlmkuza), \quad vdlmkuza \geq 1
\]
for \( wcntoqrb \) as a function of \( vdlmkuza \) satisfying the initial conditions \( wcntoqrb(1)=wcntoqrb^{\prime}(1)=\ldots= \) \( wcntoqrb^{(khqrezop-1)}(1)=0 \), where \( pzejxlym \) is continuous and
\[
fyhjqska \equiv vdlmkuza \frac{d}{d vdlmkuza}
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. We first show that
\[
fyhjqska(fyhjqska-1)(fyhjqska-2) \cdots(fyhjqska-khqrezop+1) wcntoqrb=vdlmkuza^{khqrezop} \frac{d^{khqrezop} wcntoqrb}{d vdlmkuza^{khqrezop}}
\]

We prove this by induction on \( khqrezop \). It is true for \( khqrezop=1 \). Assume (1) is true for \( khqrezop=abdyeuql \). Since polynomials in the operator \( fyhjqska \) commute with one another, we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
fyhjqska(fyhjqska-1)(fyhjqska-2) \cdots(fyhjqska-abdyeuql & +1)(fyhjqska-abdyeuql) wcntoqrb \\
& =(fyhjqska-abdyeuql) fyhjqska(fyhjqska-1)(fyhjqska-2) \cdots(fyhjqska-abdyeuql+1) wcntoqrb \\
& =\left(vdlmkuza \frac{d}{d vdlmkuza}-abdyeuql\right) vdlmkuza^{abdyeuql} \frac{d^{abdyeuql} wcntoqrb}{d vdlmkuza^{abdyeuql}} \\
& =vdlmkuza^{abdyeuql+1} \frac{d^{abdyeuql+1} wcntoqrb}{d vdlmkuza^{abdyeuql+1}}
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus (1) is proved by induction for all \( khqrezop \).
The differential equation is thus
\[
vdlmkuza^{khqrezop} wcntoqrb^{(khqrezop)}=pzejxlym(vdlmkuza), \quad vdlmkuza \geq 1
\]
and the solution can obviously be obtained by applying the integral operator \( \int_{1}^{vdlmkuza} \) khqrezop times to the function \( pzejxlym(vdlmkuza) vdlmkuza^{-khqrezop} \). However, it is possible to collapse the khqrezop-fold integration to a single integration as follows:

One of the standard forms of Taylor's theorem is
\[
\begin{aligned}
wcntoqrb(vdlmkuza)=wcntoqrb(mnvrstci)+(vdlmkuza-mnvrstci) wcntoqrb^{\prime}(mnvrstci)+\cdots+\frac{(vdlmkuza-mnvrstci)^{khqrezop-1}}{(khqrezop-1)!} & wcntoqrb^{(khqrezop-1)}(mnvrstci) \\
& +\int_{mnvrstci}^{vdlmkuza} \frac{(vdlmkuza-sguvbrki)^{khqrezop-1}}{(khqrezop-1)!} wcntoqrb^{(khqrezop)}(sguvbrki) d sguvbrki
\end{aligned}
\]
if \( wcntoqrb^{(khqrezop)} \) is continuous. (See, for example, Thomas, Calculus and Analytic Geometry, alternate ed., Addison-Wesley, Reading, Mass., 1972, page 814.) In this case, taking \( mnvrstci=1 \) and using (2) and the initial conditions, we have
\[
wcntoqrb(vdlmkuza)=\int_{1}^{vdlmkuza} \frac{(vdlmkuza-sguvbrki)^{khqrezop-1}}{(khqrezop-1)!} \cdot \frac{pzejxlym(sguvbrki)}{sguvbrki^{khqrezop}} d sguvbrki
\]

It is easy to check by direct differentiation that the function defined by this integral does indeed satisfy the differential equation (2) and the initial conditions.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $d\ge 2$ and $n\ge 1$ be fixed integers.  
Inside the positive orthant  
\[
\Omega:=\{x\in(0,\infty)^{d}\;:\;\|x\|:=(x_{1}^{2}+\dots +x_{d}^{2})^{1/2}\ge 2\}\subset\mathbb R^{d},
\]
introduce the smooth polar splitting  
\[
\rho(x):=\|x\|,\qquad w(x):=\frac{x}{\|x\|}\quad (\text{so }w(x)\in\Sigma),\qquad 
\Sigma:=\{w\in(0,\infty)^{d}\;:\;\|w\|=1\}.
\]

Denote by  
\[
\Delta:=x_{1}\frac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}}+\dots +x_{d}\frac{\partial}{\partial x_{d}}
\]
the $d$-dimensional Euler (dilation) operator.  

Assume that  
\[
h\in C^{\,n}(\Omega)
\]
is given.  Determine an explicit \emph{single one-dimensional} definite-integral formula for the unique function  
\[
y\in C^{\,n}(\Omega)
\]
that satisfies the \emph{Euler poly-PDE}  
\[
\boxed{\;
\Delta(\Delta-1)(\Delta-2)\dots(\Delta-n+1)\,y(x)=h(x)\quad (x\in\Omega)
\;}
\tag{1}
\]
together with the \emph{radial trace conditions}  
\[
\boxed{\;
\bigl(\Delta^{\,k}y\bigr)(2w)=0\quad\forall\,w\in\Sigma,\;k=0,1,\dots ,n-1.
\;}
\tag{2}
\]

Your final formula must contain only a single integral whose kernel is written \emph{completely explicitly} (no iterated or implicit integrals).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout, $d,n,\Omega,\rho,w,\Sigma$ and $\Delta$ are as in the statement.

\smallskip
\textbf{Step 1. Reduction to one dimension.}  
Fix $w\in\Sigma$ and consider the ray  
\[
R_{w}:=\{rw\;:\;r\ge 2\}.
\]
Write each point on $R_{w}$ in logarithmic form
\[
x=rw=2e^{\sigma}w,\qquad \sigma:=\ln\frac{r}{2}\ge 0. \tag{3}
\]

For any $C^{1}$-function $F$ the chain rule yields  
\[
\frac{d}{d\sigma}F\!\bigl(2e^{\sigma}w\bigr)=
\sum_{j=1}^{d}\partial_{j}F\!\bigl(2e^{\sigma}w\bigr)\,2e^{\sigma}w_{j}
=\bigl(x\cdot\nabla F\bigr)(x)=\Delta F(x). \tag{4}
\]
Hence, along each ray the Euler operator acts as the ordinary derivative  
\[
D:=\frac{d}{d\sigma}.
\]
Put   
\[
L_{n}:=D(D-1)\dots(D-n+1). \tag{5}
\]

Define one-variable functions  
\[
Y_{w}(\sigma):=y\bigl(2e^{\sigma}w\bigr),\qquad
H_{w}(\sigma):=h\bigl(2e^{\sigma}w\bigr). \tag{6}
\]
With \eqref{4}, the PDE \eqref{1} becomes, for each fixed $w$,
\[
L_{n}Y_{w}(\sigma)=H_{w}(\sigma)\qquad(\sigma\ge 0). \tag{7}
\]

\smallskip
\textbf{Step 2. Initial data transported from \eqref{2}.}  
By \eqref{6} the trace conditions give  
\[
Y_{w}^{(k)}(0)=0\qquad(k=0,1,\dots ,n-1). \tag{8}
\]

\smallskip
\textbf{Step 3. Construction and \emph{correct} proof of the Green kernel.}  

For $s\in\mathbb R$ set  
\[
g_{n}(s):=H(s)\,\frac{\bigl(e^{s}-1\bigr)^{\,n-1}}{(n-1)!}, \qquad 
H(s)=\begin{cases}0,&s<0,\\ 1,&s>0.\end{cases} \tag{9}
\]

\emph{Auxiliary identity.} For every $n\ge 1$ one has, in the sense of
distributions on $\mathbb R$,
\[
\boxed{\,L_{n}g_{n}=\delta_{0}\,}. \tag{10}
\]

We supply a rigorous proof that avoids the incorrect integration-by-parts
argument criticised in the review.

\medskip
\emph{Lemma.} For every $n\ge 1$,
\[
(D-n)\,g_{\,n+1}=g_{n}\qquad\text{in }\mathscr D'(\mathbb R). \tag{11}
\]

\emph{Proof of the Lemma.}  
Because $g_{\,n+1}$ is supported on $[0,\infty)$ we may restrict to that
half-line.  On $(0,\infty)$ put $u(s):=e^{s}-1$.  Then
$g_{\,n+1}(s)=u(s)^{n}/n!$ and
\[
Dg_{\,n+1}
=\frac{d}{ds}\bigl(u^{n}/n!\bigr)
=\frac{n}{n!}\,e^{s}\,u^{\,n-1}
=\frac{1}{(n-1)!}\bigl(u+1\bigr)u^{\,n-1}.
\]
Consequently
\[
(D-n)g_{\,n+1}
=\frac{1}{(n-1)!}\Bigl[(u+1)u^{\,n-1}-u^{\,n}\Bigr]
=\frac{u^{\,n-1}}{(n-1)!}=g_{n}\quad(s>0).
\]
At $s<0$ both sides of \eqref{11} vanish, and at $s=0$ no additional
$\delta$-terms appear because $u^{\,n}$ vanishes to order $n$ at $s=0$,
so the multiplication by $H(s)$ kills the derivative of $H$ arising from
$D$.  Hence \eqref{11} holds distributionally. \hfill$\square$

\medskip
\emph{Induction proof of \eqref{10}.}  
For $n=1$ we have $g_{1}=H$ and $L_{1}=D$, so $L_{1}g_{1}=DH=\delta_{0}$.

Assume $L_{n}g_{n}=\delta_{0}$ for some $n\ge 1$.  
Using \eqref{11} one obtains
\[
L_{\,n+1}g_{\,n+1}
=D(D-1)\dots(D-n)\,g_{\,n+1}
=L_{n}\bigl[(D-n)g_{\,n+1}\bigr]
=L_{n}g_{n}
=\delta_{0}.
\]
Thus \eqref{10} holds for all $n$ by induction. \hfill$\square$

\medskip
From \eqref{9} we also record the initial behaviour  
\[
g_{n}^{(k)}(0^{+})=
\begin{cases}
0,&k<n-1,\\
1,&k=n-1.
\end{cases}
\tag{12}
\]

\smallskip
\textbf{Step 4. Solving the ODE \eqref{7}-\eqref{8}.}  
Because the data \eqref{12} coincide exactly with \eqref{8},
the causal Green representation provides, for $\sigma\ge 0$,
\[
Y_{w}(\sigma)=\int_{0}^{\sigma}g_{n}(\sigma-\tau)\,H_{w}(\tau)\,d\tau. \tag{13}
\]

\smallskip
\textbf{Step 5. Rewriting the one-dimensional integral in $(t,x)$-variables.}  
Put $\tau=\ln\frac{t}{2}$ so that $t=2e^{\tau}$ and $d\tau=\frac{dt}{t}$;
similarly $\sigma=\ln\frac{r}{2}$ with $r=\rho(x)$.  Substituting
\eqref{13} gives, for $x=rw$,
\[
y(rw)=\int_{2}^{r}K_{n}\!\Bigl(\frac{r}{t}\Bigr)\,
       h(tw)\,\frac{dt}{t},\tag{14}
\]
where the \emph{explicit single-variable kernel} is
\[
K_{n}(z)=\frac{(z-1)^{\,n-1}}{(n-1)!},\qquad z\ge 1. \tag{15}
\]

Finally, writing $r=\rho(x)$ and $w=w(x)$ for arbitrary $x\in\Omega$
yields the announced formula
\[
\boxed{\;
 y(x)=\frac{1}{(n-1)!}\int_{2}^{\rho(x)}
 \Bigl(\frac{\rho(x)}{t}-1\Bigr)^{\,n-1}\,
 h\!\bigl(t\,w(x)\bigr)\,\frac{dt}{t}
\;}. \tag{16}
\]

\smallskip
\textbf{Step 6. Verification.}

(a) \emph{PDE \eqref{1}.}  
For fixed $w$ formula \eqref{16} coincides with \eqref{13}; applying
$L_{n}$ in the $\sigma$-variable and using \eqref{10} gives \eqref{7},
hence \eqref{1}.

(b) \emph{Boundary conditions \eqref{2}.}  
The initial behaviour \eqref{12} fed into \eqref{13} gives
$Y_{w}^{(k)}(0)=0$ for $k<n$, i.e.\ \eqref{2}.

(c) \emph{Regularity.}  
Because $h\in C^{\,n}(\Omega)$ and $\rho(x)\ge 2$,
standard differentiation under the integral sign shows
$y\in C^{\,n}(\Omega)$.

(d) \emph{Uniqueness.}  
For each ray $R_{w}$ the inhomogeneous ODE \eqref{7} with data \eqref{8}
has a unique $C^{\,n}$ solution; two
$C^{\,n}$ solutions of \eqref{1},\eqref{2} therefore coincide on every
ray and hence on all of $\Omega$.

\smallskip
\textbf{Conclusion.}  
Formula \eqref{16} with kernel \eqref{15} furnishes the unique classical
$C^{\,n}$ solution of the higher-dimensional boundary-value problem
\eqref{1}-\eqref{2}.  The $n$-fold operation has been reduced to a
\emph{single} explicitly written integral, and the previously faulty
Step~3 has been rigorously rectified.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension: the problem is promoted from an ordinary differential equation on [2,∞) to a partial differential equation on the d-dimensional positive orthant.  
2. Additional structure: the total Euler operator couples all variables; solving requires decomposing ℝ⁺ᵈ into dilation “rays’’ (radial-angular coordinates) and showing that Δ becomes ∂/∂σ on those rays.  
3. Multiple interacting concepts: one must blend PDE theory, change-of-variables in several dimensions, and the classical one-variable Euler identity to collapse an n-fold integral to a single integral.  
4. Deeper theory: the proof hinges on understanding the orbits of the multiplicative group action, showing uniqueness via a family of parameterised ODEs, and carefully translating boundary data between coordinate systems.  
5. Greater computational load: turning (1) into (6), integrating n times with weighted kernels, and re-expressing the result back in the original variables all add substantive algebraic and conceptual steps absent from the original one-dimensional problem.  

In sum, the enhanced variant extends the core “Euler-type operator with shifted factors’’ idea into a genuinely multidimensional setting with non-trivial geometry and PDE techniques, making it significantly harder than both the original and the previous kernel versions.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $d\ge 2$ and $n\ge 1$ be fixed integers.  
Inside the positive orthant  
\[
\Omega:=\{x\in(0,\infty)^{d}\;:\;\|x\|:=(x_{1}^{2}+\dots +x_{d}^{2})^{1/2}\ge 2\}\subset\mathbb R^{d},
\]
introduce the smooth polar splitting  
\[
\rho(x):=\|x\|,\qquad w(x):=\frac{x}{\|x\|}\quad (\text{so }w(x)\in\Sigma),\qquad 
\Sigma:=\{w\in(0,\infty)^{d}\;:\;\|w\|=1\}.
\]

Denote by  
\[
\Delta:=x_{1}\frac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}}+\dots +x_{d}\frac{\partial}{\partial x_{d}}
\]
the $d$-dimensional Euler (dilation) operator.  

Assume that  
\[
h\in C^{\,n}(\Omega)
\]
is given.  Determine an explicit \emph{single one-dimensional} definite-integral formula for the unique function  
\[
y\in C^{\,n}(\Omega)
\]
that satisfies the \emph{Euler poly-PDE}  
\[
\boxed{\;
\Delta(\Delta-1)(\Delta-2)\dots(\Delta-n+1)\,y(x)=h(x)\quad (x\in\Omega)
\;}
\tag{1}
\]
together with the \emph{radial trace conditions}  
\[
\boxed{\;
\bigl(\Delta^{\,k}y\bigr)(2w)=0\quad\forall\,w\in\Sigma,\;k=0,1,\dots ,n-1.
\;}
\tag{2}
\]

Your final formula must contain only a single integral whose kernel is written \emph{completely explicitly} (no iterated or implicit integrals).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout, $d,n,\Omega,\rho,w,\Sigma$ and $\Delta$ are as in the statement.

\smallskip
\textbf{Step 1. Reduction to one dimension.}  
Fix $w\in\Sigma$ and consider the ray  
\[
R_{w}:=\{rw\;:\;r\ge 2\}.
\]
Write each point on $R_{w}$ in logarithmic form
\[
x=rw=2e^{\sigma}w,\qquad \sigma:=\ln\frac{r}{2}\ge 0. \tag{3}
\]

For any $C^{1}$-function $F$ the chain rule yields  
\[
\frac{d}{d\sigma}F\!\bigl(2e^{\sigma}w\bigr)=
\sum_{j=1}^{d}\partial_{j}F\!\bigl(2e^{\sigma}w\bigr)\,2e^{\sigma}w_{j}
=\bigl(x\cdot\nabla F\bigr)(x)=\Delta F(x). \tag{4}
\]
Hence, along each ray the Euler operator acts as the ordinary derivative  
\[
D:=\frac{d}{d\sigma}.
\]
Put   
\[
L_{n}:=D(D-1)\dots(D-n+1). \tag{5}
\]

Define one-variable functions  
\[
Y_{w}(\sigma):=y\bigl(2e^{\sigma}w\bigr),\qquad
H_{w}(\sigma):=h\bigl(2e^{\sigma}w\bigr). \tag{6}
\]
With \eqref{4}, the PDE \eqref{1} becomes, for each fixed $w$,
\[
L_{n}Y_{w}(\sigma)=H_{w}(\sigma)\qquad(\sigma\ge 0). \tag{7}
\]

\smallskip
\textbf{Step 2. Initial data transported from \eqref{2}.}  
By \eqref{6} the trace conditions give  
\[
Y_{w}^{(k)}(0)=0\qquad(k=0,1,\dots ,n-1). \tag{8}
\]

\smallskip
\textbf{Step 3. Construction and \emph{correct} proof of the Green kernel.}  

For $s\in\mathbb R$ set  
\[
g_{n}(s):=H(s)\,\frac{\bigl(e^{s}-1\bigr)^{\,n-1}}{(n-1)!}, \qquad 
H(s)=\begin{cases}0,&s<0,\\ 1,&s>0.\end{cases} \tag{9}
\]

\emph{Auxiliary identity.} For every $n\ge 1$ one has, in the sense of
distributions on $\mathbb R$,
\[
\boxed{\,L_{n}g_{n}=\delta_{0}\,}. \tag{10}
\]

We supply a rigorous proof that avoids the incorrect integration-by-parts
argument criticised in the review.

\medskip
\emph{Lemma.} For every $n\ge 1$,
\[
(D-n)\,g_{\,n+1}=g_{n}\qquad\text{in }\mathscr D'(\mathbb R). \tag{11}
\]

\emph{Proof of the Lemma.}  
Because $g_{\,n+1}$ is supported on $[0,\infty)$ we may restrict to that
half-line.  On $(0,\infty)$ put $u(s):=e^{s}-1$.  Then
$g_{\,n+1}(s)=u(s)^{n}/n!$ and
\[
Dg_{\,n+1}
=\frac{d}{ds}\bigl(u^{n}/n!\bigr)
=\frac{n}{n!}\,e^{s}\,u^{\,n-1}
=\frac{1}{(n-1)!}\bigl(u+1\bigr)u^{\,n-1}.
\]
Consequently
\[
(D-n)g_{\,n+1}
=\frac{1}{(n-1)!}\Bigl[(u+1)u^{\,n-1}-u^{\,n}\Bigr]
=\frac{u^{\,n-1}}{(n-1)!}=g_{n}\quad(s>0).
\]
At $s<0$ both sides of \eqref{11} vanish, and at $s=0$ no additional
$\delta$-terms appear because $u^{\,n}$ vanishes to order $n$ at $s=0$,
so the multiplication by $H(s)$ kills the derivative of $H$ arising from
$D$.  Hence \eqref{11} holds distributionally. \hfill$\square$

\medskip
\emph{Induction proof of \eqref{10}.}  
For $n=1$ we have $g_{1}=H$ and $L_{1}=D$, so $L_{1}g_{1}=DH=\delta_{0}$.

Assume $L_{n}g_{n}=\delta_{0}$ for some $n\ge 1$.  
Using \eqref{11} one obtains
\[
L_{\,n+1}g_{\,n+1}
=D(D-1)\dots(D-n)\,g_{\,n+1}
=L_{n}\bigl[(D-n)g_{\,n+1}\bigr]
=L_{n}g_{n}
=\delta_{0}.
\]
Thus \eqref{10} holds for all $n$ by induction. \hfill$\square$

\medskip
From \eqref{9} we also record the initial behaviour  
\[
g_{n}^{(k)}(0^{+})=
\begin{cases}
0,&k<n-1,\\
1,&k=n-1.
\end{cases}
\tag{12}
\]

\smallskip
\textbf{Step 4. Solving the ODE \eqref{7}-\eqref{8}.}  
Because the data \eqref{12} coincide exactly with \eqref{8},
the causal Green representation provides, for $\sigma\ge 0$,
\[
Y_{w}(\sigma)=\int_{0}^{\sigma}g_{n}(\sigma-\tau)\,H_{w}(\tau)\,d\tau. \tag{13}
\]

\smallskip
\textbf{Step 5. Rewriting the one-dimensional integral in $(t,x)$-variables.}  
Put $\tau=\ln\frac{t}{2}$ so that $t=2e^{\tau}$ and $d\tau=\frac{dt}{t}$;
similarly $\sigma=\ln\frac{r}{2}$ with $r=\rho(x)$.  Substituting
\eqref{13} gives, for $x=rw$,
\[
y(rw)=\int_{2}^{r}K_{n}\!\Bigl(\frac{r}{t}\Bigr)\,
       h(tw)\,\frac{dt}{t},\tag{14}
\]
where the \emph{explicit single-variable kernel} is
\[
K_{n}(z)=\frac{(z-1)^{\,n-1}}{(n-1)!},\qquad z\ge 1. \tag{15}
\]

Finally, writing $r=\rho(x)$ and $w=w(x)$ for arbitrary $x\in\Omega$
yields the announced formula
\[
\boxed{\;
 y(x)=\frac{1}{(n-1)!}\int_{2}^{\rho(x)}
 \Bigl(\frac{\rho(x)}{t}-1\Bigr)^{\,n-1}\,
 h\!\bigl(t\,w(x)\bigr)\,\frac{dt}{t}
\;}. \tag{16}
\]

\smallskip
\textbf{Step 6. Verification.}

(a) \emph{PDE \eqref{1}.}  
For fixed $w$ formula \eqref{16} coincides with \eqref{13}; applying
$L_{n}$ in the $\sigma$-variable and using \eqref{10} gives \eqref{7},
hence \eqref{1}.

(b) \emph{Boundary conditions \eqref{2}.}  
The initial behaviour \eqref{12} fed into \eqref{13} gives
$Y_{w}^{(k)}(0)=0$ for $k<n$, i.e.\ \eqref{2}.

(c) \emph{Regularity.}  
Because $h\in C^{\,n}(\Omega)$ and $\rho(x)\ge 2$,
standard differentiation under the integral sign shows
$y\in C^{\,n}(\Omega)$.

(d) \emph{Uniqueness.}  
For each ray $R_{w}$ the inhomogeneous ODE \eqref{7} with data \eqref{8}
has a unique $C^{\,n}$ solution; two
$C^{\,n}$ solutions of \eqref{1},\eqref{2} therefore coincide on every
ray and hence on all of $\Omega$.

\smallskip
\textbf{Conclusion.}  
Formula \eqref{16} with kernel \eqref{15} furnishes the unique classical
$C^{\,n}$ solution of the higher-dimensional boundary-value problem
\eqref{1}-\eqref{2}.  The $n$-fold operation has been reduced to a
\emph{single} explicitly written integral, and the previously faulty
Step~3 has been rigorously rectified.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension: the problem is promoted from an ordinary differential equation on [2,∞) to a partial differential equation on the d-dimensional positive orthant.  
2. Additional structure: the total Euler operator couples all variables; solving requires decomposing ℝ⁺ᵈ into dilation “rays’’ (radial-angular coordinates) and showing that Δ becomes ∂/∂σ on those rays.  
3. Multiple interacting concepts: one must blend PDE theory, change-of-variables in several dimensions, and the classical one-variable Euler identity to collapse an n-fold integral to a single integral.  
4. Deeper theory: the proof hinges on understanding the orbits of the multiplicative group action, showing uniqueness via a family of parameterised ODEs, and carefully translating boundary data between coordinate systems.  
5. Greater computational load: turning (1) into (6), integrating n times with weighted kernels, and re-expressing the result back in the original variables all add substantive algebraic and conceptual steps absent from the original one-dimensional problem.  

In sum, the enhanced variant extends the core “Euler-type operator with shifted factors’’ idea into a genuinely multidimensional setting with non-trivial geometry and PDE techniques, making it significantly harder than both the original and the previous kernel versions.
